coverage: representative slice of the shipped 229-quality vocabulary: 48 qualities (24 curated + 24 randomly-sampled extra), 2 samples each
verdict: A tight, reliable template generator: all 96 samples are grammatical, on-topic (every one names the requested quality), and wholesomely professional. The main limits are appropriateness on the extra (non-curated) qualities and lexical repetition; several abstract qualities collapse onto ill-fitting impacts, and some quality pairs produce byte-identical outputs.
honest_discrepancy: The deterministic Python appropriateness metric reads 100% (impact is in the quality's allowed family set), but the LLM judge rates appropriateness 0.78 because the 10 semantic families are COARSE — some allowed impacts don't truly fit every quality in their family. The deterministic metric overstates semantic quality; reported plainly.
weaknesses: Reflective/steady qualities (groundedness, introspection, centeredness, balance, presence) collapse onto 'turned a hard week around' (composure family), which fits none well.
'charity' -> 'saved us a lot of time' (generosity family) is a clear mismatch.
Low structural variety: 'really shows' and a few impacts overused; some quality pairs give byte-identical outputs.
comparison: Shipped 229-quality model: 0.83 overall. The earlier curated-only 24-quality model scored 0.90 — hand-mapped impacts are more apt than coarse semantic families. Next target: split the coarse families (e.g. composure into calm vs resilient) and prune ill-fitting impacts.
note: LLM-as-judge qualitative benchmark on the actual shipped model, recorded honestly alongside the deterministic Python metrics. Not a capability-class claim.
